name: Excel & Sheets
blurb: Formulas, lookups, pivots, Power Query — spreadsheet mastery.

lessons.title: Cell References: Relative vs Absolute ($)
lessons.concept:
Every spreadsheet formula is really a set of directions. When you put =B2*C2 in cell D2, Excel doesn't store the literal addresses "B2" and "C2" — it stores relative directions: "multiply the cell two to my left by the cell one to my left." So when you copy that formula down a salary sheet or a GST invoice, the directions travel with it and each row calculates itself from its own neighbours. This behaviour is called a *relative reference*, and it is the single feature that lets one formula do the work of a thousand.

But sometimes you do NOT want a reference to move. Imagine a GST column where every line item must be multiplied by the rate sitting in one fixed cell, say H1 holding 18%. If you copy =B2*H1 down, the H1 also drifts to H2, H3 (which are empty) and your tax collapses to zero. The fix is an *absolute reference*: put dollar signs before the column letter and the row number — =B2*$H$1. The $ locks that address so it never shifts, no matter where you copy it.

There is also a middle setting called a *mixed reference*, where you lock only the column ($H1) or only the row (H$1). This matters the day you build a two-way table — like a rate card where products run down the rows and cities run across the columns — because each half of the reference has to behave differently. In both Microsoft Excel and Google Sheets the rules are identical, and you can cycle a selected reference through the four combinations by pressing F4 (this works in both apps; on a Mac laptop use Fn+F4), or by typing the $ signs yourself.
lessons.example: You have a price list in column B and want to add 18% GST using the rate stored once in cell H1. In C2 you write =B2*(1+$H$1) and drag it down 500 rows. B2 becomes B3, B4, B5 automatically, but $H$1 stays put — so every row reads the same 18% and you change the whole sheet's tax by editing one cell.
lessons.code:
H1: 0.18            (the GST rate, stored once)

C2: =B2*(1+$H$1)    → item price + 18% GST
Copy C2 down:
C3: =B3*(1+$H$1)    ← B moved, $H$1 stayed locked
C4: =B4*(1+$H$1)

Mixed reference for a 2-way rate table
(products in col A, months across row 1):
B2: =B$1*$A2        ← row 1 locked, column A locked
lessons.tryit: Build a 5-row product sheet: prices in B2:B6 and the GST rate 0.18 in H1. In C2 write =B2*(1+$H$1), copy it down to C6, then change H1 to 0.12 and confirm all five totals update at once. Finally break it on purpose: rewrite C2 as =B2*(1+H1) without the dollars, copy down, and watch rows 3–6 give wrong answers.

mistakes.m: Copying a formula that points to a single fixed cell (like a rate or exchange value) without locking it, so it drifts to empty cells and returns 0 or errors.
mistakes.fix: Put $ on the fixed cell before copying: =B2*$H$1, not =B2*H1. Press F4 on the reference to add the dollars quickly.

mistakes.m: Locking everything with $ out of habit (=$B$2*$C$2) so the formula can't be copied down and every row shows the first row's result.
mistakes.fix: Only lock what must stay fixed. Values that should change per row (like each item's price) stay relative; only the shared constant gets $.

mistakes.m: Confusing when to lock the column vs the row in a two-way table, so the grid fills with repeated or wrong numbers.
mistakes.fix: Lock the direction that must not move: reference the header row with $ on the row (B$1) and the label column with $ on the column ($A2). Test one corner cell before copying across.

links.t: Microsoft: Switch between relative, absolute and mixed references
Google Sheets: ADDRESS function (absolute vs relative $ reference modes)

quiz.q: You copy =A2*B1 from C2 down to C3. B1 holds a shared discount rate. What does C3 become?
quiz.why: Both references are relative, so copying down one row shifts both by one row: A2→A3 and B1→B2. Because B2 is likely empty, the result is wrong — which is exactly why the shared rate should have been locked as $B$1 before copying.
quiz.approach: Copying a formula shifts every un-locked reference by the same offset you moved. Track each reference separately, then check whether the shift lands on the data you intended.

quiz.q: Which formula, when copied down a column, always applies the single GST rate stored in H1?
quiz.why: The rate is in row 1 and must never drift to another row as you copy DOWN, so the row number must be locked: H$1. Locking the column too ($H$1) also works, but among these options only H$1 protects the row from the downward copy.
quiz.approach: Identify the direction of the copy (down = rows change). Lock the part of the reference that lies in that changing direction so it can't drift onto empty cells.

quiz.q: What is the effect of pressing F4 while the cursor is on a reference in an Excel formula?
opts: It cycles the reference through A1 → $A$1 → A$1 → $A1
It converts the formula to a value
quiz.why: F4 cycles a selected reference through the four lock states (relative, fully absolute, mixed-row, mixed-column), letting you add or remove $ signs without typing them. Recalculating the workbook is on F9, not F4.
quiz.approach: Remember F4 as the 'toggle the dollars' key. When you're unsure which $ pattern you need, place the cursor on the reference and tap F4 to preview each option.

lessons.title: Core Formulas: SUM, IF, COUNTIF, SUMIF, IFERROR
lessons.concept:
Five functions cover the bulk of everyday office work, and once they click, most reports become fast. SUM adds a range — =SUM(B2:B31) totals a month of daily sales. IF makes a decision: =IF(D2>=40,"Full","Short") checks whether an employee logged 40 hours and stamps a status. Notice the shape: IF(condition, value-if-true, value-if-false). The condition is any test that comes out TRUE or FALSE, and Excel picks one of the two outcomes.

COUNTIF and SUMIF add a filter to counting and adding. COUNTIF(range, criteria) counts how many cells match — =COUNTIF(C2:C100,"Gurugram") tells you how many orders shipped to Gurugram. SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range) adds up the numbers that belong to matching rows — =SUMIF(C2:C100,"Gurugram",D2:D100) totals only the Gurugram revenue. The criteria can be text ("Paid"), a number (5000), or a comparison written as text (">5000"). The text match ignores case, and you can use wildcards — * for any run of characters, ? for a single character — so "Gur*" would match both "Gurugram" and "Gurgaon".

IFERROR is the safety net. Real data is messy: a lookup misses, a division hits a blank, and your clean report suddenly shows #N/A or #DIV/0!. IFERROR(formula, value-if-error) catches that and shows something tidy instead — =IFERROR(B2/C2,0) returns 0 rather than an ugly error when C2 is empty. All five behave identically in Excel and Google Sheets, so the same muscle memory works in both. A word of caution on IFERROR: it hides ALL errors, including genuine mistakes in your formula, so add it only once the formula itself is proven correct.
lessons.example: An attendance sheet lists each employee's total present days in column D. You want three things: total present days across the team =SUM(D2:D51); how many employees crossed 22 days =COUNTIF(D2:D51,">=22"); and a per-person status =IF(D2>=22,"Regular","Review"). Add =IFERROR(...) around any division (like present ÷ working days) so blank rows don't break the sheet.
lessons.code:
=SUM(B2:B31)                          total of a range
=IF(D2>=40,"Full","Short")            decision on hours
=COUNTIF(C2:C100,"Gurugram")          count matching text
=COUNTIF(D2:D51,">=22")               count with a comparison
=SUMIF(C2:C100,"Gurugram",D2:D100)    conditional total (₹)
=IFERROR(B2/C2, 0)                     replace errors with 0

Combine them:
=IFERROR(SUMIF(A:A,"Paid",C:C)/COUNTIF(A:A,"Paid"), 0)
 → average value of Paid invoices, safe if none exist
lessons.tryit: Make a 10-row sales sheet: City in column A, Amount (₹) in column B. Write =SUM(B2:B11) for the grand total, =COUNTIF(A2:A11,"Mumbai") to count Mumbai rows, and =SUMIF(A2:A11,"Mumbai",B2:B11) for Mumbai's revenue. Then add a column C with =IFERROR(B2/SUM($B$2:$B$11),0) to show each row's share of total, and confirm no error appears even if you blank one amount.

mistakes.m: Giving SUMIF a sum_range that starts on a different row or is a different size from the criteria range, e.g. =SUMIF(A2:A100,"Paid",C5:C50). Excel and Sheets ignore the size you typed and re-anchor the sum_range from its top-left cell to match the criteria range's shape (here effectively C5:C103), so it silently adds the wrong rows.
mistakes.fix: Line up the criteria range and sum_range in both starting cell and height: =SUMIF(A2:A100,"Paid",C2:C100). When their top-left cells and row counts match, the range you see is the range that actually gets summed.

mistakes.m: Forgetting the quotes on a text comparison criterion, e.g. =COUNTIF(D2:D51,>=22), which throws an error.
mistakes.fix: Wrap comparison criteria in quotes: ">=22". Only a bare cell reference or a plain number can be left unquoted.

mistakes.m: Wrapping a whole formula in IFERROR too early, so a real typo or wrong reference is silently hidden and you trust a wrong number.
mistakes.fix: Build and verify the formula first. Add IFERROR only to handle expected, harmless errors like division by an empty cell — after you know the core logic is right.

quiz.q: Which formula totals only the sales where column A equals "Paid", with amounts in column C?
quiz.why: SUMIF takes (criteria_range, criteria, sum_range). The status is in column A so that is the criteria range, and the money in column C is the sum range: =SUMIF(A2:A100,"Paid",C2:C100). Option A reverses the ranges, and COUNTIF only counts.
quiz.approach: Map each argument to its column: where does the condition live, and where do the numbers to add live? Criteria range first, then the criterion, then the sum range.

quiz.q: =IFERROR(B2/C2, "-") is entered and C2 is empty. What appears in the cell?
quiz.why: Dividing by an empty (zero) cell would normally give #DIV/0!, but IFERROR catches that error and returns its second argument, the dash. IFERROR replaces the error with whatever you specify.
quiz.approach: Read IFERROR as 'try the first thing; if it errors, show the second thing instead'. Decide what the error result should look like and put that as the second argument.

quiz.q: You need to count employees with 22 or more present days in D2:D51. Which is correct?
quiz.why: A comparison criterion must be a text string, so the operator and number go inside quotes: ">=22". Without quotes it errors; "22" counts only exact 22s; and SUMIF would add the days, not count the people.
quiz.approach: For 'how many rows match', reach for COUNTIF. When the criterion is a comparison, always quote the whole operator-plus-number as text.

lessons.title: Lookups: VLOOKUP, XLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH
lessons.concept:
Half of office spreadsheet work is really "find the row that matches this ID, and bring back one value from it." That is a lookup. Say you have an employee master sheet (Emp ID, Name, Department, CTC) and a separate attendance sheet with only Emp IDs. To pull each person's name across, you use a lookup keyed on Emp ID. The classic tool is VLOOKUP(lookup_value, table, column_number, FALSE). The final FALSE means "exact match" — and for ID-style lookups you almost always want FALSE, because TRUE quietly assumes your table is sorted and can return the wrong neighbour.

VLOOKUP has two real weaknesses: it can only look to the *right* of the key column, and the column_number is a hard-coded count that breaks the moment someone inserts a column. XLOOKUP, the modern replacement in Excel and Google Sheets, fixes both. Its shape is XLOOKUP(lookup_value, lookup_array, return_array, [if_not_found]) — you point at the key column and the result column directly, so it can look left or right, survives inserted columns, and lets you supply a clean "Not found" message in one step instead of wrapping in IFERROR.

INDEX-MATCH is the third approach and still worth knowing because it works in every version and every tool. INDEX(return_range, MATCH(lookup_value, lookup_range, 0)) reads as "MATCH finds the position of the ID, INDEX returns the value at that position." The 0 in MATCH means exact match, the same idea as VLOOKUP's FALSE. If your organisation is on a current Excel or on Google Sheets, prefer XLOOKUP for new work; keep INDEX-MATCH for older files and reach for VLOOKUP only when a colleague's sheet already uses it.
lessons.example: Attendance sheet has Emp ID in A2. The master table sits in Sheet 'Master' with IDs in column A and names in column B. VLOOKUP way: =VLOOKUP(A2, Master!A:D, 2, FALSE) returns the name (2nd column). XLOOKUP way, clearer and future-proof: =XLOOKUP(A2, Master!A:A, Master!B:B, "Not found"). If someone inserts a column into the master, the XLOOKUP keeps working while the VLOOKUP's '2' now points at the wrong column.
lessons.code:
VLOOKUP (exact match — note the FALSE):
=VLOOKUP(A2, Master!A:D, 2, FALSE)

XLOOKUP (modern; look either direction, built-in fallback):
=XLOOKUP(A2, Master!A:A, Master!B:B, "Not found")

INDEX-MATCH (works everywhere; 0 = exact):
=INDEX(Master!B:B, MATCH(A2, Master!A:A, 0))

Guard an old VLOOKUP against #N/A:
=IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2, Master!A:D, 2, FALSE), "Not found")
lessons.tryit: Create a Master sheet with Emp ID (A), Name (B), Department (C). On a second sheet, type an Emp ID in A2 and fetch the name three ways: =VLOOKUP(A2,Master!A:C,2,FALSE), =XLOOKUP(A2,Master!A:A,Master!B:B,"Not found"), and =INDEX(Master!B:B,MATCH(A2,Master!A:A,0)). Now insert a new column between A and B in Master and see which formulas still return the right name — this shows you why XLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH are safer.

mistakes.m: Omitting the last VLOOKUP argument or setting it to TRUE, so Excel does an approximate match on unsorted data and returns a plausible but wrong value.
mistakes.fix: Always end an ID/text lookup with FALSE (or 0) for exact match: =VLOOKUP(A2, table, 2, FALSE). Reserve TRUE for deliberately sorted range lookups like tax slabs.

mistakes.m: Hard-coding the VLOOKUP column number (like 4) and then inserting or deleting a column, which shifts the data and returns the wrong field.
mistakes.fix: Use XLOOKUP or INDEX-MATCH, which reference the result column directly and survive inserted columns. If you must use VLOOKUP, recheck the column number after any structural change.

mistakes.m: Trying to VLOOKUP a value that sits to the LEFT of the key column — VLOOKUP only counts columns rightward from the key, so the value is simply unreachable and you get #N/A, a #REF!, or the wrong field.
mistakes.fix: To return a column on the left of the key, use XLOOKUP or INDEX-MATCH, which take separate lookup and return ranges and can pull a value from either side of the lookup column.

quiz.q: Why can =VLOOKUP(A2, Master!A:D, 3, FALSE) suddenly return the wrong field one day even though nobody edited the formula?
opts: Because FALSE turned off exact match
Because someone inserted or deleted a column in the Master table, shifting what column 3 points to
Because VLOOKUP expires after a set time
Because A2 became text instead of a number
quiz.why: The '3' is a fixed count of columns from the key. Insert or delete a column in the table and the data that used to be 3rd is now 2nd or 4th, so VLOOKUP faithfully returns the wrong field. XLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH avoid this by pointing at the result column directly.
quiz.approach: When a hard-coded column index is involved, ask what happens to that count if the table's shape changes. Structural edits are the classic cause of a 'formula that broke itself'.

quiz.q: Your key (Emp ID) is in column C and the Name you want is in column A, to its LEFT. Which will work?
quiz.why: VLOOKUP can only return columns to the right of its key, so it can't fetch a value in column A when the key is in column C. XLOOKUP takes separate lookup and return arrays and can return a column on either side, so it handles a leftward lookup cleanly.
quiz.approach: Check the physical position of the result relative to the key. If the answer sits left of the key, rule out VLOOKUP and use XLOOKUP or INDEX-MATCH.

quiz.q: In =INDEX(B:B, MATCH(A2, A:A, 0)), what is the job of the 0?
opts: It returns the value zero if not found
It tells MATCH to find an exact match
It rounds the result to 0 decimals
quiz.why: The third argument of MATCH is the match type; 0 means exact match (the equivalent of VLOOKUP's FALSE). MATCH returns the position of the exact hit, and INDEX then pulls the value at that position from column B.
quiz.approach: Read INDEX-MATCH inside-out: MATCH first (find the row position, exact = 0), then INDEX (return the value at that position). The match-type argument controls exact vs approximate.

lessons.title: Cleaning Data & Pivot Tables: From Messy to Report
lessons.concept:
Raw data downloaded from a billing system, an HRMS, or a bank statement is rarely clean. You'll find stray spaces (" Mumbai " that won't match "Mumbai"), duplicate rows, names glued together in one column, and numbers stored as text. Three quick tools handle most of it. TRIM removes extra spaces — =TRIM(A2) turns " Rahul  Sharma " into "Rahul Sharma" (it also collapses double internal spaces to single). One catch: TRIM only strips the normal space character, not the non-breaking space (character 160) that often rides along with text copied from web pages or PDFs — for those, pair it with SUBSTITUTE or a Find & Replace. Remove Duplicates (Excel: Data tab; Sheets: Data > Data cleanup) strips repeated rows in one click. Text to Columns (Excel: Data tab; Sheets: Data > Split text to columns) splits "Rahul,Sales,Gurugram" into three tidy columns using a delimiter like a comma. Always clean on a copy, because these steps overwrite your data.

Once data is clean, a Pivot Table summarises thousands of rows in seconds without writing a single formula. A pivot answers questions like "total ₹ sales by city" or "headcount by department" by dragging fields into four zones: Rows (what to group by, e.g. City), Columns (a second grouping, e.g. Month), Values (the number to aggregate, e.g. Sum of Amount), and Filters (to slice, e.g. only Paid invoices). The same drag-and-drop model exists in Excel (Insert > PivotTable) and Google Sheets (Insert > Pivot table).

Two habits make pivots reliable. First, your source must be a clean rectangle: one header row, no blank rows or merged cells, one fact per row. Second, when the underlying data changes, an Excel pivot does NOT auto-update — you must Refresh it (right-click > Refresh). Google Sheets pivots do refresh on their own, but in both tools any rows added outside the original source range stay invisible until you extend that range (turning the source into a Table avoids this). A Pivot Chart is simply a chart wired to the pivot, so it moves as you filter and refresh — the fastest route to a live dashboard tile. For repeatable monthly cleaning of the same messy export, Power Query (Excel) can record the steps once and replay them, and Google Sheets offers macros for the same idea; but for beginners the manual tools above cover the daily work.
lessons.example: You get a monthly sales export: columns City, Product, Amount, Status — 4,000 rows, some cities typed as " delhi " and "Delhi", plus duplicate rows from a double download. Clean it: TRIM the city column, Remove Duplicates, then insert a Pivot Table with City in Rows, Sum of Amount in Values, and Status in Filters set to "Paid". In under a minute you have paid revenue per city — and a pivot chart beside it for the review deck.
lessons.code:
Cleaning:
=TRIM(A2)                   remove stray/extra spaces
=PROPER(TRIM(A2))           tidy spaces + Title Case names
Data > Remove Duplicates    delete repeated rows (on a copy)
Data > Text to Columns      split "Rahul,Sales,Gurugram" by comma

Pivot Table field layout for "Paid ₹ by City":
  Rows    → City
  Values  → Sum of Amount
  Filter  → Status = "Paid"

After editing source data:  right-click pivot > Refresh
lessons.tryit: Take (or type) 15 rows with City, Product, Amount, Status — deliberately add two duplicate rows and a couple of cities with extra spaces like " Pune ". Clean the city column with =TRIM(), use Remove Duplicates, then insert a Pivot Table with City in Rows and Sum of Amount in Values. Add Status to the Filters zone and set it to "Paid". Finally, change one amount in the source, then Refresh the pivot and confirm the total updates.

mistakes.m: Building a pivot on data with blank rows, merged cells, or two header rows, which makes fields go missing or grouping behave oddly.
mistakes.fix: Give the pivot one clean rectangular table: a single header row, no blank/merged cells, one record per row. Convert the range to an Excel Table (Ctrl+T) so it stays tidy and expands automatically.

mistakes.m: Changing the source data and assuming the pivot updated, then reporting stale totals.
mistakes.fix: An Excel pivot caches its data — after any change to the source, right-click the pivot and choose Refresh. Verify a known total before sharing the report.

mistakes.m: Running Remove Duplicates or Text to Columns directly on the only copy of your data and destroying rows or overwriting an adjacent column.
mistakes.fix: Always work on a duplicate of the sheet. For Text to Columns, make sure the columns to the right are empty first, since the split writes into them.

links.t: Microsoft: Create a PivotTable to analyze worksheet data
Google Sheets: Create and use pivot tables

quiz.q: You added 200 new rows to your source data, but the pivot table still shows the old total. What is the most likely reason?
opts: The pivot caches its data and needs to be refreshed (and its range may need extending)
The pivot converted the numbers to text
quiz.why: A pivot can show stale totals because Excel caches the source and only updates on Refresh, and because rows added outside the original source range are excluded until you extend it (using a Table avoids this). Pivots have no 1,000-row limit, and TRIM doesn't delete rows.
quiz.approach: When a summary looks stale after a data change, suspect caching first. Refresh, then confirm the source range actually covers the new rows.

quiz.q: " Delhi " (with spaces) and "Delhi" are being counted as two different cities in your report. Which fixes it best?
opts: Apply =TRIM() to the city values, then use the cleaned column
quiz.why: Leading and trailing spaces make otherwise-identical text mismatch. TRIM strips those extra spaces so " Delhi " becomes "Delhi" and both collapse into one group. Widening, re-fonting or sorting don't change the underlying text.
quiz.approach: When identical-looking labels don't group together, suspect invisible characters. Clean the text with TRIM (or Find & Replace); if they still won't merge, a non-breaking space (character 160) may be hiding and needs SUBSTITUTE.

quiz.q: In a Pivot Table, to get 'total Amount for each City', which zones do City and Amount go into?
quiz.why: The field you group BY goes to Rows (City), and the number you aggregate goes to Values set to Sum (Amount). Putting Amount in Rows would list every amount instead of totalling, and Filters only slices the data.
quiz.approach: Ask 'group by what?' → Rows, and 'measure what?' → Values. Match each field to the question: the category defines rows, the metric fills values.

lessons.title: The Dollar Sign That Saves Your Weekend: References & Core Formulas
lessons.concept:
Every formula you copy down a column lives or dies by how its references behave. A plain reference like B2 is *relative* — it shifts as you copy. Copy =B2*C2 from row 2 to row 3 and it becomes =B3*C3, which is usually exactly what you want. But the moment your formula points to a single fixed cell — a GST rate in F1, a target in B1 — relative behaviour betrays you: the reference drifts to F2, F3, and your numbers turn to nonsense.

The fix is the dollar sign. $F$1 is *absolute* — locked to column F and row 1 no matter where you copy it. $F1 locks the column but lets the row move; F$1 locks the row but lets the column move (these *mixed* references are the secret behind clean multiplication grids). In Excel, select the reference and press F4 to cycle A1 → $A$1 → A$1 → $A1. Google Sheets uses the same $ syntax and the same F4 shortcut (fn+F4 on a Mac).

On top of references sit the five workhorse formulas of Indian office life: SUM adds a range, IF makes a decision, COUNTIF counts the cells in a range that match a condition, SUMIF adds up the values whose rows match a condition, and IFERROR catches the #DIV/0! and #N/A errors that make a dashboard look broken. Master these five plus the dollar sign and you can build 80% of a salary or GST sheet without ever touching an advanced feature.
lessons.example: You have 500 invoices in column B (base amount). The GST rate 18% sits in cell F1. Write =B2*F1 and copy down: row 3 becomes =B3*F2 — but F2 is empty, so GST silently drops to zero and your totals collapse. Lock the rate as =B2*$F$1 and every single row correctly reads the 18% in F1, no matter how far you drag.
lessons.code:
GST SHEET  (rate 18% in one locked cell)
  B = Base amount   |   $F$1 = 0.18 (GST rate)
  C2: =B2*$F$1          -> GST amount     1000 -> 180
  D2: =B2+C2            -> Invoice total  1180
  Copy C2:D2 down 500 rows -- $F$1 never drifts.

CORE FIVE  (State in col H, Amount in col D, Hours in col G)
  =SUM(D2:D501)                     grand total (Rs)
  =IF(G2>=40,"Present","Short")     attendance flag
  =COUNTIF(H2:H501,"Delhi")         # of Delhi invoices
  =SUMIF(H2:H501,"Delhi",D2:D501)   Rs total from Delhi
  =IFERROR(D2/G2,0)                 Rs per hour, 0 if blank
lessons.tryit: Build a 10-row invoice sheet. Put the GST rate (18%) in a single cell, F1. In column C compute GST with an absolute reference to $F$1, in column D the invoice total, then copy both formulas down all 10 rows and confirm the rate cell never drifts. Finally, use SUMIF to total the invoice value for one state and COUNTIF to count how many invoices came from that state.

mistakes.m: Forgetting the $ on a rate or target cell, so copying the formula down makes the reference 'drift' onto empty cells and returns 0 or wrong totals.
mistakes.fix: Click the reference in the formula bar and press F4 to lock it as $F$1 before you copy down.

mistakes.m: Building long nested IF(ISERROR(...)) chains just to hide #DIV/0! or #N/A.
mistakes.fix: Wrap the whole formula in IFERROR(formula, 0) or IFERROR(formula, "") — it is shorter and catches every error type at once.

mistakes.m: Confusing COUNTIF (counts matching cells) with SUMIF (adds a value column for matching rows).
mistakes.fix: Ask 'how many?' -> COUNTIF; ask 'total of rupees?' -> SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range).

links.t: Switch between relative, absolute and mixed references — Microsoft

quiz.q: You typed =B2*F1 in C2 and copied it down. C3 shows a wrong, too-small value. F1 holds the GST rate. What went wrong?
opts: F1 needed to be formatted as a percentage first
The reference F1 drifted to F2 (empty) because it was not absolute
The * operator only multiplies when it is wrapped inside SUM
Excel rounds any cell holding a percentage down to zero
quiz.why: A relative reference shifts when copied down, so F1 became F2 in the next row. F2 is empty (treated as 0), so the GST multiplied to zero. Locking it as $F$1 pins every row to the rate cell. Number formatting and rounding have nothing to do with it.
quiz.approach: When a copied formula breaks only from the second row onward, suspect a reference that should have been made absolute with $.

quiz.q: Which formula counts how many invoices in H2:H501 are from 'Mumbai'?
quiz.why: COUNTIF(range, criteria) counts cells that match the text. COUNT only tallies numbers, SUMIF adds values instead of counting, and a bare IF cannot process a whole range into a single count.
quiz.approach: 'How many match?' is always COUNTIF; 'total value of the matches?' is SUMIF.

quiz.q: What does =IFERROR(D2/G2, 0) return when G2 is empty?
opts: A blank cell that still shows an error triangle
quiz.why: Dividing by an empty (zero) cell throws #DIV/0!, but IFERROR catches any error and returns the fallback you supplied — here, 0. If D2/G2 had NOT errored, you would simply get its result.
quiz.approach: Read IFERROR(value, fallback): if value errors you get the fallback, otherwise you get value.

lessons.title: Stop Scrolling: VLOOKUP, XLOOKUP & INDEX-MATCH
lessons.concept:
The single most common analyst task is *joining* two tables: you have an Employee ID and need the name, department, or CTC that lives in a master sheet. Doing this by eye across 4,000 rows is how errors — and late nights — happen. Lookup functions automate the match.

VLOOKUP is the old faithful: =VLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, col_index_num, FALSE). It searches the *first* column of a table and returns a value from a column to its right. Two things trip people up: always end with FALSE (or 0) for an exact match — TRUE does an approximate match that silently returns wrong data on unsorted lists — and remember col_index_num is a fixed number, so inserting a column inside the table breaks it. VLOOKUP also cannot look to the *left* of the key column.

XLOOKUP (Excel 365 / 2021, and now Google Sheets) fixes all of that: =XLOOKUP(lookup_value, lookup_array, return_array, "Not found"). It looks in any direction, returns an exact match by default, and lets you supply your own 'not found' text instead of #N/A. When XLOOKUP is unavailable (Excel 2016/2019), INDEX-MATCH is the timeless combo: =INDEX(return_range, MATCH(lookup_value, lookup_range, 0)). MATCH finds the *position* of the key, INDEX pulls the value at that position — it works left or right and survives column inserts.
lessons.example: A master sheet lists Employee ID (col F), Name (col G), Department (col H). Your attendance sheet only has the ID in A2, so =VLOOKUP(A2, F:H, 3, FALSE) fetches the Department. But if HR later inserts a 'Location' column between Name and Department, column 3 now holds Location — your formula quietly returns the wrong field with no error at all. XLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH, which point at the return column directly, never suffer this silent bug.
lessons.code:
Master table: F=EmpID, G=Name, H=Department

VLOOKUP  =VLOOKUP(A2, $F$2:$H$5000, 2, FALSE)   -> Name
         (col 2 of the table = Name; FALSE = exact match)

XLOOKUP  =XLOOKUP(A2, $F$2:$F$5000, $G$2:$G$5000, "Not found")
         -> Name, exact by default, clean fallback text

INDEX-MATCH  =INDEX($G$2:$G$5000, MATCH(A2, $F$2:$F$5000, 0))
         -> Name; survives reordered / inserted columns

Look LEFT (ID in G, need value in F) -- VLOOKUP cannot:
         =XLOOKUP(A2, $G$2:$G$5000, $F$2:$F$5000)
lessons.tryit: Create a 15-row master table with Employee ID, Name, and CTC. On a second sheet, given only three IDs, fetch each employee's CTC three ways — once with VLOOKUP (FALSE for exact match), once with XLOOKUP, and once with INDEX-MATCH. Then insert a new column into the middle of the master table and observe which of the three formulas breaks.

mistakes.m: Leaving out the FALSE (exact-match) argument in VLOOKUP, so it does an approximate match and returns the wrong record on an unsorted list.
mistakes.fix: Always end VLOOKUP with FALSE or 0 unless you deliberately want a banded/approximate lookup on sorted data.

mistakes.m: Hard-coding col_index_num, then inserting a column inside the table so VLOOKUP silently returns the wrong field.
mistakes.fix: Prefer XLOOKUP or INDEX-MATCH, which reference the return column directly instead of by a position number.

mistakes.m: Getting #N/A and assuming the data is missing, when it is really a type mismatch (ID stored as text '1001' vs number 1001) or stray spaces.
mistakes.fix: TRIM the keys and make both sides the same data type; only wrap in IFERROR after you have ruled out a real mismatch.

quiz.q: Your VLOOKUP worked for weeks, then HR inserted a column in the master table and now it returns the wrong field. Why?
opts: VLOOKUP stops working once a table passes 1,000 rows
col_index_num is a fixed position number, so inserting a column shifts the data it points to
Inserting a column automatically flips the FALSE argument to TRUE
VLOOKUP searches the first row, not the first column, so new columns confuse it
quiz.why: VLOOKUP returns whatever sits at the hard-coded column number counted from the key column. Insert a column and that position now holds different data, with no error shown. Row count and the exact-match flag are irrelevant; XLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH reference the return range directly and survive the edit.
quiz.approach: If a lookup breaks right after someone edits the table's structure, suspect a position-based reference.

quiz.q: Which function can return a value from a column to the LEFT of the lookup column?
quiz.why: VLOOKUP (like HLOOKUP) can only return a value on one side of its key — to the right of the lookup column — and a negative col_index_num simply errors. XLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH take the lookup and return ranges independently, so either direction works.
quiz.approach: Need to look left, or want insert-proof formulas? Reach for XLOOKUP or INDEX-MATCH.

quiz.q: In =INDEX($G$2:$G$5000, MATCH(A2, $F$2:$F$5000, 0)), what is the job of MATCH(..., 0)?
opts: It adds up all the values in column F
It returns the position of A2 within F2:F5000 using an exact match
It returns the name in column F that matches A2
It checks whether A2 exists and returns TRUE or FALSE
quiz.why: MATCH returns the position of A2 within the lookup range (say, the 40th row of F2:F5000), and the 0 forces an exact match. INDEX then pulls the value sitting at that same position in the return range. MATCH hands back a position number — not the value itself, and not TRUE/FALSE.
quiz.approach: Read INDEX-MATCH inside-out: MATCH finds 'which position', INDEX fetches 'the value at that position'.

lessons.title: Messy In, Clean Out: Text, Dates & Data Cleaning
lessons.concept:
Real Indian business data is filthy: names with double spaces and trailing blanks copied from a portal, dates stored as text like '05-04-2026' that refuse to sort, a 'Full Name' column that should be two, and duplicate rows from three merged exports. Before any pivot or lookup will behave, you clean.

The text toolkit: TRIM removes leading, trailing, and repeated internal spaces — though not the invisible non-breaking space (CHAR 160) that web copies carry; for that use SUBSTITUTE(A2, CHAR(160), " "). LEFT, RIGHT, MID slice strings; LEN measures them; UPPER/LOWER/PROPER fix casing; & or TEXTJOIN stitches them back. To split a 'Rajesh Kumar' column, use Data → Text to Columns (delimited by space) in Excel, or SPLIT() in Google Sheets. To remove duplicate rows, use Data → Remove Duplicates in Excel, or UNIQUE() / Data → Data cleanup in Sheets.

Dates are numbers in disguise — Excel stores 18-Jul-2026 as a serial number, which is exactly why real dates sort and calculate while text dates don't. DATEDIF(start, end, "Y") gives completed years (tenure, age); NETWORKDAYS(start, end, holidays) counts working days for attendance; EOMONTH(date, 0) returns month-end for salary cut-offs; TEXT(date, "dd-mmm-yyyy") formats for reports. Layer conditional formatting (Home → Conditional Formatting) to flag attendance below 40 hours in red, and named ranges (Formulas → Name Manager) to write =B2*GST_RATE instead of =B2*$F$1 for readability.
lessons.example: You paste 2,000 employee names from a vendor portal. Some read 'Priya  Sharma ' with a trailing space and a double space in the middle. A VLOOKUP on these names fails mysteriously with #N/A. Run =TRIM(A2) and 'Priya  Sharma ' becomes a clean 'Priya Sharma' — and suddenly every lookup matches, because to the computer those two strings were never equal.
lessons.code:
CLEAN TEXT
=TRIM(A2)                       strip extra/leading/trailing spaces
=PROPER(TRIM(A2))               "rajesh KUMAR" -> "Rajesh Kumar"
=SUBSTITUTE(A2,CHAR(160)," ")   kill non-breaking spaces from web
=LEFT(A2, FIND(" ",A2)-1)       first name (up to the first space)
=TRIM(MID(A2, FIND(" ",A2), 99))  everything after the first space

DATES
=DATEDIF(C2, TODAY(), "Y")      completed years of service
=NETWORKDAYS(D2, E2, Holidays)  working days in a month
=EOMONTH(TODAY(), 0)            last day of this month (payroll)
=TEXT(F2, "dd-mmm-yyyy")        17-Jul-2026 as clean report text

NAMED RANGE + CONDITIONAL FORMAT
Name Manager: GST_RATE = Sheet1!$F$1  ->  =B2*GST_RATE
Rule (formula): =$G2<40  -> fill red   (attendance short)
lessons.tryit: Take a column of messy full names (some with extra spaces and mixed casing) and produce three clean columns: Full Name (TRIM + PROPER), First Name, and Last Name. Then add a Date of Joining column, compute each person's completed years of service with DATEDIF, and apply conditional formatting to highlight anyone with under 2 years in one colour.

mistakes.m: Assuming a date that looks right is a real date — text dates like '05/04/2026' left-align and won't sort or feed DATEDIF/NETWORKDAYS.
mistakes.fix: Check alignment (real dates right-align by default) or convert with DATEVALUE / Text to Columns, then format the cells as a date.

mistakes.m: Running Remove Duplicates on a filtered or partial selection, deleting the wrong rows or missing dupes hidden by the filter.
mistakes.fix: Clear all filters and select the whole table first, and keep a backup copy — Remove Duplicates permanently deletes rows.

mistakes.m: TRIM not fixing spaces copied from a website because they are non-breaking spaces (CHAR 160), not normal spaces.
mistakes.fix: Wrap with SUBSTITUTE(A2, CHAR(160), " ") before TRIM, or use CLEAN to strip other invisible control characters.

quiz.q: A VLOOKUP on employee names keeps returning #N/A even though the name clearly exists in both sheets. The most likely cause is:
opts: The name has trailing, leading, or double spaces on one side
VLOOKUP cannot match text values, only numbers
The lookup column must be sorted alphabetically for names to match
VLOOKUP is case-sensitive, so the capitalisation differs
quiz.why: Extra spaces make 'Priya Sharma ' and 'Priya Sharma' unequal to the computer, so the exact match fails. TRIM both sides (and check for CHAR 160) and it succeeds. VLOOKUP handles text fine, does not need a sorted list for an exact (FALSE) match, and is NOT case-sensitive — so casing alone would not cause #N/A.
quiz.approach: When two values look identical but won't match, suspect invisible spaces or a text-vs-number mismatch first.

quiz.q: Which function returns the number of completed years between a joining date in C2 and today?
quiz.why: DATEDIF with unit 'Y' returns completed years between two dates — ideal for tenure or age. NETWORKDAYS counts working days, EOMONTH returns a month-end date, and LEN counts characters.
quiz.approach: For a gap measured in whole years/months/days between two dates, use DATEDIF with the right unit code.

quiz.q: You copied data from a web portal and TRIM still leaves a gap-looking character at the start. Why?
opts: Your version of Excel has a broken TRIM function
The character is a non-breaking space (CHAR 160), which TRIM does not remove
The cell is formatted as text, which disables TRIM
You must press Enter twice to force TRIM to recalculate
quiz.why: TRIM was designed to remove only the standard 7-bit space (CHAR 32). Web content often carries the non-breaking space (CHAR 160), which TRIM leaves untouched — replace it first with SUBSTITUTE(A2, CHAR(160), " "). A text cell format does not disable TRIM.
quiz.approach: If TRIM seems to 'fail', test the leftover character with CODE() — a result of 160 means a non-breaking space.

lessons.title: From Rows to Decisions: Pivot Tables, Power Query & Dashboards
lessons.concept:
A pivot table turns 50,000 raw invoice rows into a one-glance summary — total rupees by state, by month, by salesperson — without a single formula. In Excel: select the data, Insert → PivotTable, then drag fields into four zones: Filters, Rows, Columns, and Values. Drop 'State' in Rows and 'Invoice Amount' in Values (set to Sum) and you instantly have state-wise revenue. Google Sheets works the same way via Insert → Pivot table. Pivot charts (or a chart built on the pivot in Sheets) make it visual, and slicers add click-to-filter buttons for a dashboard feel.

The catch every beginner hits: a pivot table does not update itself when the source data changes — you must right-click → Refresh (or Data → Refresh All). And if you add new rows below the source, the pivot may not include them unless your source is a proper Excel Table (Ctrl+T) or a dynamic range. Value Field Settings let you switch a field between Sum, Count, Average, or 'Show Values As → % of Grand Total' — the difference between 'how much' and 'how many'.

When cleaning and combining data becomes repetitive, Power Query (Excel's Data → Get & Transform) is the upgrade: it records every step — remove columns, change types, split, unpivot, merge two files — into a refreshable recipe, so next month you just hit Refresh instead of redoing it all. It is proper ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) inside Excel. Google Sheets has no Power Query but offers Connected Sheets and IMPORTRANGE for related goals. For light automation, Excel offers macros (Record Macro / VBA) and Office Scripts, while Sheets offers recorded Macros and Apps Script — record once, replay a routine monthly report with one click.
lessons.example: Every month you download regional sales as five separate files, delete the same junk columns, fix the date type, and stack them. Done by hand that is an hour of tedium and typos. Built once in Power Query as an 'append + transform' query, next month's refresh takes ten seconds — point it at the new files and click Refresh All.
lessons.code:
PIVOT -- build a state-wise sales summary
1. Ctrl+T on the source (makes a Table so new rows auto-include)
2. Insert -> PivotTable
3. Rows:    State
   Columns: Month
   Values:  Sum of Invoice Amount   (Value Field Settings -> Sum)
   Filters: Financial Year
4. Source changed? Right-click -> Refresh (pivots never auto-update)

VALUE FIELD tricks
- Sum -> total Rs      Count -> number of invoices
- Show Values As -> % of Grand Total   (share by state)

POWER QUERY (Excel: Data -> Get Data)
Extract   -> From Folder / From Workbook
Transform -> Remove Columns, Change Type, Split, Unpivot, Merge
Load      -> to a sheet, or straight into a PivotTable
Next month: Data -> Refresh All  (the recipe re-runs itself)

Google Sheets equivalents
Pivot:    Insert -> Pivot table
Combine:  IMPORTRANGE / Connected Sheets
Automate: Extensions -> Macros -> Record macro (Apps Script)
lessons.tryit: Make a 100-row sales table with columns State, Month, Salesperson, and Invoice Amount. Convert it to a Table (Ctrl+T), then build a pivot table showing total invoice amount by State in Rows and Month in Columns. Change the value field to show each cell as a % of Grand Total, add a slicer on State, then add three new rows to the source and hit Refresh to confirm they flow into the pivot.

mistakes.m: Editing the source data and expecting the pivot table to update on its own, then reporting stale numbers to your manager.
mistakes.fix: Pivots never auto-refresh — right-click the pivot → Refresh, or Data → Refresh All, after any source change.

mistakes.m: Building a pivot on a fixed range like A1:E500, so rows added later are silently ignored.
mistakes.fix: Convert the source to an Excel Table (Ctrl+T) first; the pivot's source then grows automatically as rows are added.

mistakes.m: Leaving a value field as Count when you meant a total, so rupee figures look absurdly small.
mistakes.fix: Double-click the value field → Value Field Settings → choose Sum (or Average) instead of Count.

links.t: Create a PivotTable to analyze worksheet data — Microsoft
About Power Query in Excel — Microsoft

quiz.q: You changed several invoice amounts in the source data, but the pivot table still shows the old totals. What must you do?
opts: Delete and rebuild the pivot from scratch each time
Right-click the pivot and choose Refresh
Press F9 to recalculate the whole workbook
Nothing — pivot tables update live from the source
quiz.why: Pivot tables cache their data and do not update automatically. Refreshing (right-click → Refresh, or Data → Refresh All) pulls the latest values from the source. F9 recalculates worksheet formulas but does not refresh the pivot cache.
quiz.approach: Stale pivot numbers almost always mean 'not refreshed', not a broken formula.

quiz.q: What is the main advantage of Power Query for a monthly report built from several files?
opts: It automatically emails the finished report to your manager
It records the cleaning and combining steps into a refreshable recipe you re-run next month
It compresses the source files so they open faster
It removes the need for any formulas in the workbook
quiz.why: Power Query is repeatable ETL: every transform step (remove columns, change types, merge, append) is saved as a query, so next period you point it at the new data and click Refresh instead of redoing the cleanup by hand.
quiz.approach: When the same manual cleanup repeats every month, that is the signal to build it once in Power Query.

quiz.q: A pivot's Values area shows tiny numbers like 12 and 8 where you expected rupees in lakhs. The likely cause is:
opts: The value field is set to Count instead of Sum
The source column is stored as text, so the totals shrank
The pivot is showing % of Grand Total by mistake
Excel truncates any number longer than two digits
quiz.why: Count returns how many rows matched (e.g. 12 invoices), not their rupee total. Switch the field to Sum via Value Field Settings to get the amount. A text-stored source would give a blank or zero sum, and '% of Grand Total' would show percentages — different symptoms from small whole-number counts.
quiz.approach: Small round whole numbers in a Values area usually mean the aggregation is Count, not Sum.

lessons.title: Lookups that never break: INDEX-MATCH, XLOOKUP & two-way lookups
lessons.concept:
VLOOKUP is the workhorse every Indian analyst learns first, but it is fragile. It can only look rightward from the key column, and it relies on a hardcoded column number (the 3 in VLOOKUP(...,3,FALSE)) that silently breaks the moment a colleague inserts a column into your employee master or GST rate card. When you are pulling CTC against Emp Codes, or vendor rates against GSTINs, that fragility quietly corrupts an MIS report.

The professional upgrade is INDEX-MATCH and XLOOKUP. INDEX(return_range, MATCH(key, lookup_range, 0)) is a two-part machine: MATCH finds the position of your key, INDEX returns the value at that position. It can look left, survives column insertion because it references ranges (not a fragile position number), and is often faster on large sheets because it reads only the lookup and return columns rather than the whole table. XLOOKUP is the modern one-function replacement in Excel 365/2021 and Google Sheets: it is exact-match by default and has a built-in if_not_found argument so you never wrap it in IFERROR just to hide an ugly #N/A.

The real power move is the two-way lookup: nest two MATCH calls inside one INDEX to find a value at the intersection of a row AND a column. This is how you pull one employee's salary for one specific month out of a payroll or attendance grid without slicing the data first.
lessons.example: An HR analyst keeps an employee master with columns in this order: Emp Code, Name, Department, CTC. A manager sends a list of Emp Codes and wants CTC pulled in. VLOOKUP works only because CTC sits to the right of Emp Code and happens to be the 4th column. The day someone inserts a Location column before CTC (pushing CTC from the 4th to the 5th column), every VLOOKUP(...,4,FALSE) quietly returns the new Location column instead of CTC. XLOOKUP and INDEX-MATCH keep working because they point at the CTC range itself.
lessons.code:
' Modern one-function lookup (Excel 365 / Google Sheets)
=XLOOKUP(A2, EmpCode, CTC, "Not found")

' Classic, works in every Excel version — and can look LEFT
=INDEX(CTC, MATCH(A2, EmpCode, 0))

' Two-way lookup: one person's salary in one chosen month
=INDEX(B2:M50, MATCH("Ravi Kumar", A2:A50, 0), MATCH("Mar", B1:M1, 0))

' Approximate match — find the GST slab from a floor table sorted ascending
=XLOOKUP(F2, SlabFloor, GSTRate, , -1)   ' -1 = exact match or next smaller
lessons.tryit: In a copy of your team's employee master, add a column that uses XLOOKUP (or INDEX-MATCH) to pull each person's Department from their Emp Code sitting on another sheet. Then deliberately insert a new column in the middle of the master and confirm your INDEX-MATCH/XLOOKUP still returns the correct Department — whereas an old VLOOKUP with a hardcoded column index would now be wrong.

mistakes.m: Leaving off VLOOKUP's 4th argument, so it defaults to approximate match and silently returns the wrong row on unsorted data.
mistakes.fix: Always pass FALSE (or 0) as VLOOKUP's last argument for an exact match. XLOOKUP is exact by default, so switching to it removes the trap entirely.

mistakes.m: Hardcoding the column number in VLOOKUP (e.g. 4), which breaks the instant a column is inserted or deleted.
mistakes.fix: Use INDEX-MATCH or XLOOKUP, which reference the return column by range or name — not by a brittle position number.

mistakes.m: Assuming a #N/A means 'value missing' when it is really a type or space mismatch — text "1001" vs number 1001, or a trailing space.
mistakes.fix: Make both keys the same data type and TRIM stray spaces; use XLOOKUP's if_not_found (or IFERROR) only for genuinely missing values.

links.t: Look up values with VLOOKUP, INDEX, or MATCH (Microsoft Support)

quiz.q: Why do experienced analysts prefer INDEX-MATCH or XLOOKUP over VLOOKUP?
opts: They can only look to the right
They can look left and don't break when a column is inserted
They only work when the lookup column is sorted ascending
quiz.why: VLOOKUP looks only rightward and uses a fixed column number that breaks on column insertion; INDEX-MATCH and XLOOKUP reference ranges directly, can return values to the left, and — unlike VLOOKUP's approximate mode — never need the data pre-sorted for an exact match.
quiz.approach: Focus on directional flexibility and resilience to structural edits.

quiz.q: In =XLOOKUP(A2, EmpCode, CTC), what does it do by default if A2 is not found, and which match type does it use?
opts: Returns #N/A, using exact match by default
Returns the nearest value, using approximate match
quiz.why: XLOOKUP defaults to exact match (match_mode 0) and returns #N/A when nothing matches, unless you supply the optional if_not_found argument.
quiz.approach: Recall XLOOKUP's defaults: exact match, #N/A on a miss, both overridable.

quiz.q: In =INDEX(CTC, MATCH(A2, EmpCode, 0)), what is the role of the 0 in MATCH?
opts: It returns the 0th row of the range
It is the column number to return
quiz.why: MATCH's third argument sets the match type; 0 means exact match, while 1 or -1 do approximate matching on sorted data.
quiz.approach: MATCH's third argument controls match type — 0 = exact.

lessons.title: Power Query: clean and reshape messy data without formulas
lessons.concept:
Every analyst knows the monthly pain: exports from Tally, SAP or the bank arrive with merged header rows, ₹ symbols that make amounts behave as text, dates stored as strings like "12-Mar-26", duplicate invoice rows, and a separate file per branch. Cleaning all of this by hand every month is slow and error-prone. Power Query — Excel's 'Get & Transform' on the Data tab — is the answer: it records every cleaning step you take and replays the whole sequence on next month's file with a single click, Refresh.

The core moves become second nature: remove duplicates, split columns (Text-to-Columns on steroids), change data type, Trim/Clean stray spaces, and Unpivot a cross-tab (months spread across columns) into a tidy long table that pivots love. Two operations replace hours of manual work: Merge joins two tables on a key (a JOIN, like VLOOKUP but for whole tables) and Append stacks many same-shaped files into one (a UNION — perfect for combining twelve branch files). Every step is listed in the Applied Steps pane, where you can edit, reorder or delete any of them. Your raw data is never touched; the query is a clean, repeatable layer on top.

Google Sheets has no Power Query, but the mindset transfers. QUERY() runs SQL-like transformations, IMPORTRANGE pulls data in from other sheets, and Connected Sheets reaches into BigQuery. In both tools the golden rule is the same: keep raw data untouched and do your shaping in a layer you can re-run.
lessons.example: A finance executive drops a new Sales_July.xlsx into a folder each month. A Power Query built once — drop 3 junk rows, promote headers, set Amount to decimal and Date to date, remove duplicate invoices, filter out Cancelled orders, unpivot the month columns — turns every new file into a clean analysis-ready table in seconds. Next month she just points the query at Sales_August.xlsx and hits Refresh All.
lessons.code:
Power Query — Applied Steps (recorded automatically as you click):
1. Source             -> connect to Sales_July.xlsx
2. Removed Top Rows   -> drop 3 junk rows
3. Promoted Headers   -> row 1 becomes column names
4. Changed Type       -> Amount = decimal, Date = date
5. Trimmed Text       -> strip stray spaces from Vendor
6. Removed Duplicates -> on Invoice No
7. Filtered Rows      -> Status <> "Cancelled"
8. Unpivoted Columns  -> Apr/May/Jun turn into one Month + one Sales column

Google Sheets equivalent (SQL-like, one formula):
=QUERY(Raw!A1:F, "select B, sum(E) where D <> 'Cancelled' group by B label sum(E) 'Total Sales'", 1)
lessons.tryit: Take last month's raw sales or attendance export. In Excel choose Data > From Table/Range to open the Power Query Editor, then remove duplicates, set the amount and date types explicitly, and filter out blank rows. Close & Load to a new sheet. Now swap the source for a fresh file and press Refresh All — confirm every step re-applies by itself.

mistakes.m: Editing the loaded output table directly, so your fixes disappear the next time you Refresh.
mistakes.fix: Make every change inside the Power Query Editor via Applied Steps, never in the loaded sheet — the query is the single source of truth.

mistakes.m: Letting Power Query auto-detect types on the first file; a later file has one stray text value and the whole column errors out.
mistakes.fix: Set data types explicitly, and for fragile columns add a Replace Errors or clean step; re-check types after changing the source.

mistakes.m: Using QUERY() in Google Sheets with the wrong header-rows count, so the first data row is treated as a header (or vice-versa).
mistakes.fix: Pass the correct final argument (number of header rows, usually 1) and make sure the column letters match the actual range.

links.t: About Power Query in Excel (Microsoft Support)

quiz.q: You clean a file in Power Query and load it. Next month you replace the source and click Refresh All. What happens to your cleaning steps?
opts: They are lost and must be redone
They automatically re-apply to the new data
Power Query asks you to re-record each step
quiz.why: Power Query records every transformation as an Applied Step and replays the whole sequence on refresh — that repeatability is its central value.
quiz.approach: Treat Power Query as a recorded, re-runnable ETL layer, not a one-time manual edit.

quiz.q: You have 12 monthly branch files with identical columns and want them stacked into one table. Which Power Query operation?
quiz.why: Append stacks rows from multiple same-structured tables (a UNION); Merge joins columns from two tables on a key (a JOIN).
quiz.approach: Distinguish stacking rows (Append/UNION) from joining columns (Merge/JOIN).

quiz.q: A report has months as separate columns (Apr, May, Jun...). To make it tidy for a pivot — one Month column and one Value column — you use:
quiz.why: Unpivot converts wide, column-per-month data into a long attribute/value format that pivot tables and charts prefer.
quiz.approach: Wide-to-long is Unpivot; long-to-wide is Pivot.

lessons.title: Pivot tables, calculated fields, slicers & a live dashboard
lessons.concept:
A pivot table collapses thousands of rows — a year of GST invoices, a quarter of attendance logs — into a crisp cross-tab in seconds, with zero formulas. Drag a field to Rows, another to Columns, a number to Values, and Excel aggregates it (Sum, Count, Average, or a display such as % of Grand Total). For an analyst producing monthly MIS, this is the single highest-leverage skill in the tool.

Beyond drag-and-drop lies the real craft. Value Field Settings let you display '% of Grand Total' or a Running Total instead of raw rupees. Group turns raw dates into Months or Quarters. A Calculated Field adds a formula inside the pivot itself — for example GST = Taxable × 0.18 — so the number recomputes with every refresh. Slicers and Timelines are clickable, on-screen filters; connect one slicer to several pivots and a single click filters the whole page at once. A PivotChart plots the pivot and stays perfectly in sync with it.

A dashboard is simply several pivots and charts on one sheet, all wired to shared slicers and refreshed together. One rule saves careers: pivots do NOT update by themselves — after the source data changes you must Refresh (Alt+F5 for the active pivot, or Ctrl+Alt+F5 / Refresh All). GETPIVOTDATA safely lifts a single pivot number into a headline KPI tile. Google Sheets mirrors all of this with Insert > Pivot table, calculated fields, and slicers.
lessons.example: A regional sales lead wants a one-page dashboard: revenue by State (rows) crossed with Quarter (columns), a Product Category slicer, a KPI tile showing total GST collected, and a bar chart of the top 10 dealers — all filtering together the instant she clicks the slicer.
lessons.code:
Calculated Field inside a PivotTable
(PivotTable Analyze > Fields, Items & Sets > Calculated Field):
    GST @18%  =  Taxable * 0.18

Pull one pivot value into a KPI tile
(auto-generated when you click a pivot cell with GetPivotData on):
=GETPIVOTDATA("Sales", $A$3, "State", "Maharashtra", "Quarter", "Q1")

Google Sheets calculated field (pivot editor > Values > Add > Calculated field):
= Taxable * 0.18
lessons.tryit: From a table of at least 200 invoice rows (Date, State, Category, Taxable), build a pivot of Taxable by State (rows) and Category (columns). Add a Calculated Field for 18% GST, group the Date field by Quarter, insert a Slicer on Category, and add a PivotChart. Now change one source amount and press Refresh — watch the whole dashboard update in step.

mistakes.m: Editing the source data and expecting the pivot to update on its own.
mistakes.fix: A pivot is a cached snapshot — press Refresh (Alt+F5) or Refresh All (Ctrl+Alt+F5) after any source change.

mistakes.m: Building the pivot on a fixed range like A1:F500, so next month's extra rows are silently ignored.
mistakes.fix: Convert the source to an Excel Table (Ctrl+T) first; the pivot's source then expands automatically as the table grows, and new rows appear on refresh.

mistakes.m: Typing GETPIVOTDATA arguments by hand and ending up with brittle text references.
mistakes.fix: Let Excel generate the formula by clicking the target pivot cell (with 'Generate GetPivotData' turned on), then adjust only the field values you need.

links.t: Create a PivotTable to analyze worksheet data (Microsoft Support)
Create & use pivot tables (Google Docs Editors Help)
Overview of PivotTables and PivotCharts (Microsoft Support)

quiz.q: You changed several amounts in the source data, but the pivot table still shows the old totals. Why?
opts: The pivot is corrupted and must be rebuilt
Pivot tables don't recalculate automatically; you must Refresh
You need to save and reopen the file
quiz.why: A pivot table is a cached snapshot of its source, so it only reflects changes after you Refresh (Alt+F5) or Refresh All.
quiz.approach: Remember pivots are cached snapshots, not live formulas.

quiz.q: You want each state's revenue shown as a percentage of the total instead of the raw rupee figure. Where do you set this?
opts: Value Field Settings > Show Values As > % of Grand Total
A separate formula outside the pivot
quiz.why: 'Show Values As > % of Grand Total' in Value Field Settings recomputes the displayed values as shares without changing the underlying data.
quiz.approach: Percentage-of-total is a display calculation built into the Values area.

quiz.q: You want one slicer to filter three pivot tables on the same dashboard at once. What makes this possible?
opts: Slicers can only ever control one pivot
Connecting the slicer to multiple pivots via Report Connections, when they share the same data
quiz.why: A slicer's Report Connections dialog lets one slicer drive every pivot built on the same data cache.
quiz.approach: Think of a slicer as a shared filter you connect to several pivots on the same data.

lessons.title: Dynamic arrays, LET/LAMBDA & light automation
lessons.concept:
Modern Excel (365/2021) and Google Sheets calculate with whole arrays at once. A single FILTER, SORT or UNIQUE formula 'spills' a live result across as many cells as it needs — no dragging, no CTRL+SHIFT+ENTER. FILTER(Orders, Region="West") instantly returns every matching row and re-sizes itself as data changes underneath it. You reference the entire spilled block with the # operator: if the formula sits in E2, then E2# is the whole result.

Two functions make big formulas manageable. LET names intermediate results, so a long formula reads like plain steps and runs faster because each name is computed only once. LAMBDA turns a formula into your own reusable, named function: define it once in Name Manager, then call GSTAmount(D2, 0.18) like any built-in — no VBA required. Alongside these, named ranges (Formulas > Name Manager, or the Name Box) give your ₹-tables friendly names like TaxRate, so formulas read like English and don't break when rows shift around.

For genuine automation, start small. Excel's Macro Recorder captures a repetitive sequence of clicks as VBA you can replay. Office Scripts (Excel for the web, written in TypeScript) and Google Apps Script (JavaScript) do the same and can run on a schedule or behind a button. Record the monthly formatting chore once, and reuse it forever — modest automation that returns real hours on recurring MIS work.
lessons.example: An analyst maintains a live order book. The formula =SORT(FILTER(Orders, Orders[Region]="West"), 3, -1) produces a self-updating table of West-region orders sorted highest value first. The instant a new order is typed anywhere in the source, the spilled result re-sorts itself — replacing a manual filter-and-sort ritual done every single morning.
lessons.code:
Spill functions (Excel 365 / Google Sheets):
=UNIQUE(FILTER(B2:B999, C2:C999="Paid"))     ' distinct paid customers
=SORT(FILTER(Orders, Region="West"), 3, -1)  ' West orders, value high->low
=E2#                                          ' reference the whole spilled range

LET — name once, read easily:
=LET(taxable, D2, rate, 0.18, taxable + taxable*rate)   ' invoice incl. 18% GST

LAMBDA — your own reusable function (name it GSTAmount in Name Manager):
=LAMBDA(amount, rate, amount*rate)
' then call it anywhere:  =GSTAmount(D2, 0.18)
lessons.tryit: On an orders sheet, write one FILTER formula that spills all rows for a chosen region, wrap it in SORT to rank by amount descending, then reference that result elsewhere with the # spill operator. Next, create a LAMBDA named GSTAmount(amount, rate) in Name Manager and use it to compute 18% GST down a whole column with a single, readable call.

mistakes.m: A spill formula shows #SPILL! because a stray value sits in the cells it needs to fill.
mistakes.fix: Clear the blocking cells below and to the right of the formula — a dynamic array needs empty room to expand into.

mistakes.m: Placing a dynamic-array/spill formula inside an Excel Table, which does not support spilling.
mistakes.fix: Put spill formulas on the normal grid outside a Table, or reference the Table as input rather than hosting the spill inside it.

mistakes.m: Recording a macro with absolute cell selections, so it only ever works on the exact cells you recorded.
mistakes.fix: Turn on 'Use Relative References' in the Macro Recorder (or edit the VBA) so the macro applies wherever the active cell is.

links.t: Dynamic array formulas and spilled array behavior (Microsoft Support)

quiz.q: =FILTER(A2:C100, B2:B100="West") returns #SPILL!. What is the most likely cause?
opts: FILTER must be entered with Ctrl+Shift+Enter
Something is blocking the cells the result needs to fill
'West' does not exist in the data
The source range is too small
quiz.why: #SPILL! means the formula's output range is obstructed — a value, merged cell, or table is in the way; clearing the blocking cells lets the array spill. Dynamic arrays never need Ctrl+Shift+Enter.
quiz.approach: Read #SPILL! literally — the spill area is blocked.

quiz.q: What does the LET function primarily improve?
opts: It creates workbook-wide named ranges, like Name Manager
It names intermediate values, making formulas readable and computing each name once
quiz.why: LET assigns names to sub-expressions that exist only inside that single formula (not workbook-wide like Name Manager's defined names), improving readability and performance because each named value is calculated just once and then reused.
quiz.approach: Think of LET as local variables defined inside a single formula.

quiz.q: You want to reuse a custom 'GST amount' formula across the workbook and call it by a friendly name, with no VBA. Which feature?
quiz.why: LAMBDA packages a formula as a named, reusable function callable like a built-in, with no macros or VBA.
quiz.approach: A reusable named custom function with no code is LAMBDA.
